LLOYD M. HARMON, JR.
COUNTY COUNSEL

ANTHONY ALBERS
DIANE BARDSLEY
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

County of San Diego
OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL
355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2469
(619) 531-4860

DEPUTIES

AFNE HANSEN
BEVERLY B. LARA
RALPH E. SHADWELL
NATHAN C. NICHTERN
JAMES M. JOHNSON
NANCY R. NORTON
JOHN J. SANDONE
MICHAEL B. POYNOR
THOMAS J. CRAWFORD
DEBORAH L. BRISTOL
NICHOLAS STEELE
RANDALL HALL
ROBERT A. HAGER
THOMAS LINDMARK
THOMAS E. MONTGOMERY
LINDA J. MCCOLLUM
TERENCE G. DUTTON
BRUCE D. MACLEISH
LEWIS F. SCHUETTE
STEPHEN H. MAGRUDER

CHERYL K. CARTER
MARK J. RICHARDSON
VALERIE TEHAN
RICHARD HANSEN
GEORGE H. PAPPAS JR.
ANDREW P. EISEMAN
DAVID E. ROSWELL
SUSAN E. CARTER
ALLYSON A. RAMSEY
PAULINE GARCIA
PAMELA P. JONES
TERESA J. STIBBE
DEBORAH PETERSONLEE
CHERYL A. NEYERMAN
PATRICIA L. WRIGHT
R. MARK BEELEY
MIRIAM E. DELEON
RITA C. LOWELL
LAURA D. ROMANO

November 18, 1991

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attn: Luisa Menchaca

Re: Our Request for Advice
Your File No. A-91-506

Dear Ms. Menchaca:

On October 22, 1991, we requested an Advice Letter
addressing the propriety, under the Political Reform Act of 1974,
of the County of San Diego's contemplated hiring of a real estate
brokerage firm to assist it with locating and negotiating for
property to be used by the county ("County"). That request
contained three specific questions. We would like to supplement
that request with an additional question, as follows:

4. For purposes of ascertaining whether Cushman is a
"consultant" as defined in the Regulations, what criteria are
employed to determine if Cushman's rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to the County is "independent
of the control and direction of the [County] or of any [County]
official, other than normal contract monitoring"? Cal. Code
section 18700(a)(2). Specifically, what
distinguishes "normal contract monitoring" by the County of its
proposed contract with Cushman from other types of "control and
direction" of Cushman by the County? For example, will any
communication between the County and Cushman following execution
of the contract, which is not specifically related to
interpretation of the terms of the contract itself, constitute
such "control and direction" of Cushman by the County? Or is
more communication, advice, etc., possible? To expand on this
same point, one of your previous Advice Letters suggests that
Cushman would not be involved in "official decision making" if
its activities are "governed only by the provisions of [its]
contract" with the County, whereas it would be involved in
"official decision making" if the County "controls and directs"
its activities (in excess of "normal contract monitoring"). In
re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, at 71. In the context of the

Ms. Menchaca                -2-                November 18, 1991


proposed agreement, could you clarify what activities Cushman
might engage in which would be "governed only by the provisions
of [its] contract" with the County?

     Any guidance you can provide to us on this subject will be
most appreciated.  Should you have any questions, please contact
the undersigned deputy at (619) 531-6231.

                                   LLOYD M. HARMON, JR., County Counsel

                                   By [illegible]
                                      JOHN A. PARDEE, Deputy

     Department of General Services (O360)

LLOYD M. HARMON, JR.
COUNTY COUNSEL

ANTHONY ALBERS
DIANE BARDSLEY
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

Oct 20 [illegible] 31

County of San Diego
OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL
355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2469
(619) 531-4860

DEPUTIES
ARNE HANSEN
RALPH E SHADWELL
NATHAN C. MCKINNEY
MARTIN S. MCCARTHY
JOHN J. SARGONIC
MICHAEL E. ROBINSON
MICHAEL J. MCCARTHY
DESMOND L. BRUCE-LYLE
MILTON C. HILTON
ROBERT G. FARNAM
LEON LETTERMAN
THOMAS LUNDMARK
THOMAS E. MONTGOMERY
ROBERT P. MURPHY
TERENCE G. DUTTON
BRUCE G. MACLESN
BRIAN P. SCHATZ
STEPHEN R. MAGRUDER

DEPUTIES
CHERYL M. CARTER
LAURIE B. TONER-TEHAN
CALERIE TEMAN
GEORGE F. FRIEDRICH DR
LAWRENCE K. FISCHER
DAVID B. HOWARD
ALLSTON A. SWAMEY
JAMES R. COLLINS
PAMELA T. JONES
FRANK A. MCINTYRE
DEBORAH PETERSONLEE
CHERYL A. DEYERMAN
PATRICIA A. RYAN
D. MARK WESLEY
MIRIAM E. BREDINO
CARLEEN M. MURPHY
LAURA D. ROMANO

October 22, 1991

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Luisa Menchaca

Dear Ms. Menchaca,

     Pursuant to our recent telephone conversation, this letter
constitutes our request for an Advice Letter addressing the
propriety, under the Political Reform Act of 1974, of the County
of San Diego's contemplated hiring of a real estate brokerage
firm to assist it with locating and negotiating for property to
be used by the county, as described herein.

BACKGROUND

     1. The County's Intended Action. The County of San Diego
("County") frequently leases or purchases office space to house
its various departments and agencies. In order to assist it in
evaluating the local "market," and to obtain the most favorable
terms for these leases or purchases, the County's Department of
General Services is currently evaluating the possibility of
entering into an "exclusive contract" with Cushman Realty
Corporation ("Cushman") for "real estate leasing, acquisition and
consulting services." Such services would include, (i) locating
properties which generally fit criteria which the County will
have provided to Cushman for each particular project,
(ii) evaluating the respective features of properties which
the county may consider appropriate for its use,
(iii) negotiating on the County's behalf with the owners of such
properties, or their agents, with the intent of arriving at a
lease or purchase proposal containing terms which are as
favorable as possible to the County, and (iv) "overseeing" and
assisting with those actions necessary to "close" lease or
purchase transactions following the County's approval of the
terms.

     The County will retain full power to accept or reject the
terms of any lease or purchase contract negotiated on its behalf
by Cushman. In this regard, it is intended that any such

Ms. Menchaca                    -2-                    October 22, 1991


consulting contract entered into between the County and Cushman
shall clearly provide that Cushman is not empowered to act as the
County's "agent" with power to conclude a transaction on behalf
of the County, i.e., Cushman will have no power to bind the
County to the terms of any particular transaction. Rather,
Cushman will propose transactions it has negotiated for the
County's consideration, and the County will have full power and
discretion to accept or reject any such recommendation.

     Although it is contemplated that the County will be fully
involved at all times with the negotiating process, it is
unlikely that the County will have the time or resources to
independently verify all information provided to it by Cushman,
or to independently confirm that the terms presented to it by
Cushman for any particular project are the most favorable terms
available for such projects.

     Most of the leases presented by Cushman for the County's
consideration under this arrangement would first be reviewed and
evaluated by the County's Department of General Services, and
then would be presented to the County's Board of Supervisors for
its final approval. County ordinances provide, however, that
some minor leases (i.e., leases with terms of 3 years or less and
with monthly rent due of $2,500 or less) may be approved by the
Director of the County's General Services, and do not require
Board approval. Some of the leases negotiated by Cushman will,
in all likelihood, fall into this latter category.

     The proposed consulting contract between the County and
Cushman would provide for Cushman to provide services on an
on-going basis, and over the entire area of San Diego County.
Presently, the County contemplates utilizing Cushman's services
for two specific up-coming projects, (i) leasing or purchasing
space for the County's Departments of Planning and Land Use and
Public Works, and (ii) leasing court and office space for the
Superior Courts.

     Cushman only represents tenants, never owners, in its
practice; this is a County requirement to avoid the possibility
that Cushman may have represented an owner from whom the County
may lease property in another transaction, possibly creating a
conflict between Cushman's duty to such owner and its duty to
represent the County.

     The County's Board of Supervisors will probably be asked to
approve the proposed consulting contract in the next 3 to 4
weeks.

Ms. Menchaca                    -3-                    October 22, 1991


     2.  Source of Cushman's Compensation.  As in most brokerage
transactions, Cushman would receive its compensation under the
described consulting contract in the form of a commission paid by
the owner of the property, at the time of transfer of the
property to County or execution of a lease. A commission payment
is calculated as a percentage of the rent payable by the County
under a lease, or as a percentage of the purchase price paid by
the County for a property, and, again, is only payable if the
County approves the lease or purchase, as applicable. In a
normal case, the owner will have "listed" the property with
another brokerage firm (the "listing agent") with an agreed-upon
commission to be paid to that agent when the property is leased
or sold; Cushman, as the "participating broker" who provides the
tenant or purchaser (i.e., the County) to the owner and the
listing agent, will take a portion of the percentage commission
promised by the owner to the listing agent.

     The method by which Cushman would receive its compensation
under the proposed consulting agreement is "standard procedure"
in real estate transactions in California. (California Civil
Code section 2377.) Note that Civil Code section 2377 provides
that "the payment of compensation [by a particular party to a
transaction] . . . is not necessarily determinative of a
particular agency relationship between an agent and a seller or a
buyer." In order to minimize the potential for conflict of
interest, California law requires brokers to make full disclosure
of the nature of their relationship to all parties to a
transaction. (Civil Code section 2375.)

     3.  Provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and
Regulations Promulgated Pursuant Thereto Applicable to County's
Intended Action. Section 87100 of the California Government Code
provides as follows:

          "No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest."

     Section 87103 of the Government Code provides, in part, as
follows:

          "An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
     reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
     material financial effect, distinguishable from its
     effect on the public generally, on . . . ."

Ms. Menchaca                    -4-                    October 22, 1991


     "(c) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made."

     Section 18700 of the Fair Political Practices Commission
Regulations (2 Cal. Code of Regs., § 18110, et seq.)
("Regulations") provides, in part, as follows:

     "The provisions herein define terms as used in
Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as
amended, Government Code sections 87100-87312.

     "(a) "Public official at any level of state or
local government" means every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency . . . .

     "(2) "Consultant" shall include any natural
person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to a state or
local government agency, provided, however, that
"consultant" shall not include a person who:

          "(A) Conducts research and arrives at
conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or of any
agency official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and

          "(B) Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel."

     Section 18704.3 of the Regulations sets forth the disclosure
and disqualification requirements "for any public official who
receives commission income for services rendered as a . . . real
estate broker or agent." Section 18704(c)(2) and (3) of the
Regulations provide that "the sources of commission income in a

Ms. Menchaca                    -5-                    October 22, 1991


specific sale or similar transaction include for each of the
following:

    ". . .

    "(2) A real estate broker:

    "(A) The person the broker represents
in the transaction;

    "(B) If the broker receives a
commission from a transaction conducted by an
agent working under the broker's auspices,
the person represented by the agent;

    "(C) Any brokerage business entity
through which the broker conducts business;
and

    "(D) Any person who receives a finder's
or other referral fee for referring a party
to the transaction to the broker, or who
makes a referral pursuant to a contract with
the broker.

    "(3) A real estate agent:

    "(A) The broker and brokerage business
entity under whose auspices the agent works;

    "(B) The person the agent represents in
the transactions; and

    "(C) Any person who receives a finder's
or other referral fee for referring a party
to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."

Ms. Menchaca                    -6-                    October 22, 1991


                         QUESTIONS PRESENTED

     Please address the following issues in your Advice Letter:

     1.     Is Cushman a "consultant," as defined in the
Regulations, or is Cushman excluded from that category because,
with respect to its contemplated contract with the County,
Cushman "(A) [c]onducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to [its] rendition of information, advice, recommendation
or counsel independent of the control and direction of the
[County] or of any [County] official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and (B) [p]ossesses no authority with respect to any
[County] decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel," as described in section
18700(a)(2)(B) of the Regulations.

     2.     If Cushman is a "consultant" as defined in the
Regulations, therefore making it a "public official" for purposes
of Government Code section 87100, will Cushman "make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use [its] official position to
influence a governmental decision in which [it] knows or has
reason to know [it] has a financial interest," as described in
section 87100 of the Government Code, if it performs the services
describe under the contemplated consulting agreement for the
County? Please assume, for purposes of this question, that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the leases/purchase agreements
entered into by the County as a result of Cushman's activities
will have a material financial effect on the property owners from
whom the County will be leasing or purchasing property.

     3.     What effect, if any, do the Civil Code sections cited
above governing agency relationships between brokers and
property owners principals have on your answers to our questions?

     Thank you for your assistance with this matter.  Should you
have any questions, please contact the undersigned deputy at
(619) 531-6231.

                              Very truly yours,

                              LLOYD M. HARMON, JR., County Counsel

                              By
                                 JOHN A. PARDEE, Deputy

LLOYD M. HARMON, JR.
COUNTY COUNSEL

ANTHONY ALBERS
DIANE BARDSLEY
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

County of San Diego

OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL
555 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2469
(619) 531-4860

CHERYL K. CARTER
LAURA J. CASARUBIAS
VALERIE TENAN-

MEGAN E. MELODY
SUSAN L. ROSS
LAURA D. ROMANO

November 15, 1991

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Luisa Menchaca

Dear Ms. Menchaca,

Pursuant to our recent telephone conversation, this letter
constitutes our request for an Advice Letter addressing the
propriety, under the Political Reform Act of 1974, of the County
of San Diego's contemplated hiring of a real estate brokerage
firm to assist it with locating and negotiating for property to
be used by the County, as described herein.

BACKGROUND

1. The County's Intended Action. The County of San Diego
("County") frequently leases or purchases office space to house
its various departments and agencies. In order to assist it in
evaluating the local "market," and to obtain the most favorable
terms for these uses or purchases, the County's Department of
General Services is currently evaluating the possibility of
entering into an "exclusive contract" with Cushman Realty
Corporation ("Cushman") for "real estate leasing, acquisition and
consulting services." Such services would include, (i) locating
properties which generally fit criteria which the County will
have provided to Cushman for each particular project, (ii)
evaluating the respective features of "competing" properties
the County may consider appropriate for its use,
(iii) negotiating on the County's behalf with the owners of such
properties, or their agents, with the intent of arriving at a
lease or purchase proposal containing terms which are as
favorable as possible to the County, and (iv) "overseeing" and
assisting with those actions necessary to "close" lease or
purchase transactions following the County's approval of the
terms.

The County will retain full power to accept or reject the
terms of any lease or purchase contract negotiated on its behalf
by Cushman. In this regard, it is intended that any such

Ms. Menchaca                -2-                November 15, 1991


consulting contract entered into between the County and Cushman
shall clearly provide that Cushman is not empowered to act as the
County's "agent" with power to conclude a transaction on behalf
of the County, i.e., Cushman will have no power to bind the
County to the terms of any particular transaction.  Rather,
Cushman will propose transactions it has negotiated for the
County's consideration, and the County will have full power and
discretion to accept or reject any such recommendation.

     Although it is contemplated that the County will be fully
involved at all times with the negotiating process, it is
unlikely that the County will have the time or resources to
independently verify all information provided to it by Cushman,
or to independently confirm that the terms presented to it by
Cushman for any particular project are the most favorable terms
available for such project in all respects.

     Most of the leases presented by Cushman for the County's
consideration under this arrangement would first be reviewed and
evaluated by the County's Department of General Services, and
then would be presented to the County's Board of Supervisors for
its final approval.  County ordinances provide, however, that
some minor leases (i.e., leases with terms of 3 years or less and
with monthly rent due of $2,500 or less) may be approved by the
Director of the County's Department of General Services, and do
not require Board approval.  Some of the leases negotiated by
Cushman will, in all likelihood, fall into this latter category.

     The proposed consulting contract between the County and
Cushman would provide for Cushman to provide such services on an
on-going basis, and over the entire area of San Diego County.
Presently, the County contemplates utilizing Cushman's services
for two specific up-coming projects, (i) leasing or purchasing
space for the County's Departments of Planning and Land Use and
Public Works, and (ii) leasing court and office space for the
Superior Courts.

     Cushman only represents tenants, never owners, in its
practice; this is a County requirement to avoid the possibility
that Cushman may have represented an owner from whom the County
may lease property in another transaction, possibly creating a
conflict between Cushman's duty to such owner and its duty to
represent the County.

     The County's Board of Supervisors will probably be asked to
approve the proposed consulting contract in the next 3 to 4
weeks.

Ms. Menchaca                    -3-                November 15, 1991


     2.  Source of Cushman's Compensation.  As in most brokerage
transactions, Cushman would receive its compensation under the
described consulting contract in the form of a commission paid by
the owner of the property, at the time of transfer of the
property to County or execution of a lease.  A commission payment
is calculated as a percentage of the rent payable by the County
under a lease, or as a percentage of the purchase price paid by
the County for a property, and, again, is only payable if the
County approves the lease or purchase, as applicable.  In a
normal case, the owner will have "listed" the property with
another brokerage firm (the "listing agent") with an agreed-upon
commission to be paid to that agent when the property is leased
or sold; Cushman, as the "participating broker" who provides the
tenant or purchaser (i.e., the County) to the owner and the
listing agent, will take a portion of the percentage commission
promised by the owner to the listing agent.

     The method by which Cushman would receive its compensation
under the proposed consulting agreement is "standard procedure"
in real estate transactions in California.  (California Civil
Code section 2377.)  Note that Civil Code section 2377 provides
that "the payment of compensation [by a particular party to a
transaction] . . . is not necessarily determinative of a
particular agency relationship between an agent and a seller or a
buyer." In order to minimize the potential for conflict of
interest, California law requires brokers to make full disclosure
of the nature of their relationship to all parties to a
transaction.  (Civil Code section 2375.)

     3.  Provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and
Regulations Promulgated Pursuant Thereto Applicable to County's
Intended Action.  Section 87100 of the California Government Code
provides as follows:

          "No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest."

     Section 87103 of the Government Code provides, in part, as
follows:

          "An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
     reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
     material financial effect, distinguishable from its
     effect on the public generally, on . . . ."

Ms. Menchaca                    -4-                    November 15, 1991


        "(c) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made."

     Section 18700 of the Fair Political Practices Commission
Regulations (2 Cal. Code of Regs., § 18110, et seq.)
("Regulations") provides, in part, as follows:

        "The provisions herein define terms as used in
Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as
amended, Government Code sections 87100-87312.

        "(a) "Public official at any level of state or
local government" means every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency . . . .

        "(2) "Consultant" shall include any natural
person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to a state or
local government agency, provided, however, that
"consultant" shall not include a person who:

        "(A) Conducts research and arrives at
conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of
the control and direction of the agency or of any
agency official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and

        "(B) Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel."

     Section 18704.3 of the Regulations sets forth the disclosure
and disqualification requirements "for any public official who
receives commission income for services rendered as a . . . real
estate broker or agent." Section 18704(c)(2) and (3) of the
Regulations provide that "the sources of commission income in a

Ms. Menchaca                    -5-                    November 15, 1991


specific sale or similar transaction include for each of the
following:

     ". . .

     "(2) A real estate broker:

     "(A) The person the broker represents
in the transaction;

     "(B) If the broker receives a
commission from a transaction conducted by an
agent working under the broker's auspices,
the person represented by the agent;

     "(C) Any brokerage business entity
through which the broker conducts business;
and

     "(D) Any person who receives a finder's
or other referral fee for referring a party
to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a contract with
the broker.

     "(3) A real estate agent:

     "(A) The broker and brokerage business
entity under whose auspices the agent works;

     "(B) The person the agent represents in
the transactions; and

     "(C) Any person who receives a finder's
or other referral fee for referring a party
to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."

Ms. Menchaca                    -6-                    November 15, 1991


                         QUESTIONS PRESENTED

     Please address the following issues in your Advice Letter:

     1.     Is Cushman a "consultant," as defined in the
Regulations, or is Cushman excluded from that category because,
with respect to its contemplated contract with the County,
Cushman "(A) [c]onducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to [its] rendition of information, advice, recommendation
or counsel independent of the control and direction of the
[County] or of any [County] official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and (B) [p]ossesses no authority with respect to any
[County] decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel," as described in section
18700(a)(2)(B) of the Regulations.

     2.     If Cushman is a "consultant" as defined in the
Regulations, therefore making it a "public official" for purposes
of Government Code section 87100, will Cushman "make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use [its] official position to
influence a governmental decision in which [it] knows or has
reason to know [it] has a financial interest," as described in
section 87100 of the Government Code, if it performs the services
describe under the contemplated consulting agreement for the
County? Please assume, for purposes of this question, that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the leases/purchase agreements
entered into by the County as a result of Cushman's activities
will have a material financial effect on the property owners from
whom the County will be leasing or purchasing property.

     3.     What effect, if any, do the Civil Code sections cited
above governing agency relationships between brokers and
principals have on your answers to our questions?

     Thank you for your assistance with this matter.  Should you
have any questions, please contact the undersigned deputy at
(619) 531-6231.

                              Very truly yours,

                              LLOYD M. HARMON, JR., County Counsel

                              By ___________________________
                                 JOHN A. PARDEE, Deputy

L. The countvts Intended Action. The county of San Diego
(rtcountyrr) frequentl,y feases or purchases office space to house
its various departments and agencies. In order to assist it in
evatuating the local rrnarket,rr and to obtain the most favorable
terms for these leases or purchases, the countyrs Department of
General Services is currently evaluating the possibility of
entering into an |texcl-usive contractrr with cushnan Realty
Corporation (rrcushmanrt) for Itreal estate leasing, acquisition and
consutting services.rr such services woul-d include, (i) locating
properties which generally fit criteria which the County will
have provided to cushnan for each particular project,
(ii) evaluating the respective features of trcompeting" properties
the county rnay consider appropriate for its use,
(iii) 
negotiating on the countyrs behalf with the owners of such
properties, or their agents, I^rith the intent of arriving at a
lease or purchase proposal containing terms which are as
favorable as possible to the county, and (iv) rroverseeingtr and
assisti.ng with those actions necessary to rrclosert lease or
purchase transactj.ons following the Countyrs approval of the
terms .
The County will retain full power to accept or reject the
terms of any lease or purchase contract negotiated on its behalf
by Cushman. In this regard, it is intended that any such
Woulrtg of fianpiegu

1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101.2469

consulting contract entered into between the county and cushman
shall clearly provj.de that cushman is not empowered to act as the
county's rragentrt with power to conclude a transaction on behalf
of the county, i.e., 
cushrnan will have no power to bind the
county to the terms of any particular transaction. Rather,
cushman will prol:ose transactions it has negotiated for the
countyts consideration, and the county will have full- power and
discretion to accept or reject any such recommendation.
Afthough it is conternplated that the County will be fully
invol-ved at al-l- tines with the negotiating process, it is
unlikely that the county will have the tine or resources to
independently verify all inforrnation provided to it by cushman,
or to independently confirm that the terms presented to it by
cushman for any particular project are the most favorable terms
avaif abl-e for such project in all respects.
Most of the leases presented by Cushman for the Countyrs
consideration under this arrangement would first be reviewed and
evaluated by the countyts Department of ceneral services, and
then would be presented to the countyrs Board of Supervisors for
its final approval . county ordinances provide, however, that
sone minor leases (i.e., leases with terms of 3 years or less and
with nonthly rent due of $2,500 or l-ess) may be approved by the
Director of the county's Department of ceneral Services, and do
not require Board approva]. some of the l-eases negotiated by
cushman wi11, in alL likel,ihood, fa1I into this l-atter cateqory.
The proposed consulting contract betvreen the county and
cushman would provide for cushman to provide such services on an
on-going basj-s, and over the entire area of san Diego county.
Presently, the county contemptates utilizing 
Cushmanrs services
for two specific up-coming projects, (i) leasing or purchasing
space for the countyrs Departments of Planning and Land Use and
Public works, and (ii) leasing court and office space for the
Superior courts.
cushman only represents tenants, never o$/ners, in its
practicei this is a county requirement to avoid the possibility
that cushman may have represented an os/ner from whom the County
may lease property in another transaction, possj-b1y creating a
conflict between cushmanrs duty to such owner and its duty to
represent the County.
The Countyts Board of Supervisors wj-L] probably be asked to
approve the proposed consulting contract in the next 3 to 4
weeks.


l,ls . Menchaca
October 22, a997
2. source of cushmants compensation. As in most brokerage
transactions, cushman would receive its cornpensation under the
described consulting contract in the form of a commission paid by
the owner of the property, at the time of transfer of the
property to County or execution of a Lease. A commission payment
is calculated as a percentage of the rent payable by the county
under a 1ease, or as a percentage of the purchase price paid by
the County for a property, and, again, is only payabl-e if the
county approves the lease or purchase, as applicable. In a
normal case, the owner will have rrlistedrr the property with
another brokerage firm (the ttlisting agenttt) with an aqreed-upon
cornrnission to be paid to that agent when the property is l-eased
or sold; cushman, as the 'rparticipating brokerrr who provides the
tenant or purchaser (i.e., the county) to the owner and the
listing agent, will take a portion of the percentage commission
promised by the o$/ner to the listing agent.
The nethod by which cushman woul-d receive its compensation
under the proposed consulting agreement is rrstandard procedure'l
in real estate transactions in Calj.fornia. (california Civil
Code section 2377.) Note that civil code section 2377 provides
that I'the payment of cornpensation [by a particular party to a
transactionl 
is not necessarily determinative of a
particular agency relationship between an agent and a sell-er or a
buyer.tr In order to rninimize the potential for conflict of
interest, California law requires brokers to make ful-l- disclosure
of the nature of their relationship to all parties to a
transaction. 
(civil code section 2375.)
3. Provisions of the Political Reform Act of l-974 and
Regul-ations Promulqated Pursuant Thereto Appl,icable to countvrs
Intended Action. Section 8710o of the california Government code
provides as foflows:
ttNo public official 
at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in rnaking or in any
way attenpt to use his official position to influence a
governmental- decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest."
Section 87103 of the Government Code provides, in part, as
f oIlor^rs:
'tAn official 
has a financial interest i-n a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on . . . .


Ms. Menchaca
October 22, L99L
" (c) Any source of income . . . aggregating t$ro
hundred fifty dollars ($25o) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made. rl

COUNTY COUNSEL ANTHONY ALBERS DIANE BARDSLEY CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL County of San Diego OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL 355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER 1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2469 (619) 531-4860 DEPUTIES AFNE HANSEN BEVERLY B. LARA RALPH E. SHADWELL NATHAN C. NICHTERN JAMES M. JOHNSON NANCY R. NORTON JOHN J. SANDONE MICHAEL B. POYNOR THOMAS J. CRAWFORD DEBORAH L. BRISTOL NICHOLAS STEELE RANDALL HALL ROBERT A. HAGER THOMAS LINDMARK THOMAS E. MONTGOMERY LINDA J. MCCOLLUM TERENCE G. DUTTON BRUCE D. MACLEISH LEWIS F. SCHUETTE STEPHEN H. MAGRUDER CHERYL K. CARTER MARK J. RICHARDSON VALERIE TEHAN RICHARD HANSEN GEORGE H. PAPPAS JR. ANDREW P. EISEMAN DAVID E. ROSWELL SUSAN E. CARTER ALLYSON A. RAMSEY PAULINE GARCIA PAMELA P. JONES TERESA J. STIBBE DEBORAH PETERSONLEE CHERYL A. NEYERMAN PATRICIA L. WRIGHT R. MARK BEELEY MIRIAM E. DELEON RITA C. LOWELL LAURA D. ROMANO November 18, 1991 Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street Sacramento, California 95814 Attn: Luisa Menchaca Re: Our Request for Advice Your File No. A-91-506 Dear Ms. Menchaca: On October 22, 1991, we requested an Advice Letter addressing the propriety, under the Political Reform Act of 1974, of the County of San Diego's contemplated hiring of a real estate brokerage firm to assist it with locating and negotiating for property to be used by the county ("County"). That request contained three specific questions. We would like to supplement that request with an additional question, as follows: 4. For purposes of ascertaining whether Cushman is a "consultant" as defined in the Regulations, what criteria are employed to determine if Cushman's rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel to the County is "independent of the control and direction of the [County] or of any [County] official, other than normal contract monitoring"? Cal. Code section 18700(a)(2). Specifically, what distinguishes "normal contract monitoring" by the County of its proposed contract with Cushman from other types of "control and direction" of Cushman by the County? For example, will any communication between the County and Cushman following execution of the contract, which is not specifically related to interpretation of the terms of the contract itself, constitute such "control and direction" of Cushman by the County? Or is more communication, advice, etc., possible? To expand on this same point, one of your previous Advice Letters suggests that Cushman would not be involved in "official decision making" if its activities are "governed only by the provisions of [its] contract" with the County, whereas it would be involved in "official decision making" if the County "controls and directs" its activities (in excess of "normal contract monitoring"). In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, at 71. In the context of the Ms. Menchaca -2- November 18, 1991 proposed agreement, could you clarify what activities Cushman might engage in which would be "governed only by the provisions of [its] contract" with the County? Any guidance you can provide to us on this subject will be most appreciated. Should you have any questions, please contact
